votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the divisibility check, the primality predicate using trial division up to the integer square root, and the specification matching the boolean result to the predicate.
The Python code correctly implements the divisibility check, prime predicate, and specification, handling edge cases like n < 2 before calling math.isqrt to avoid errors.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq definitions for divisibility and primality testing, including the correct bounds and edge cases.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification for concrete inputs. The divides function checks divisibility (handling d=0 edge case appropriately), is_prime_pred checks if n >= 2 and no integer from 2 to sqrt(n) divides n (matching the Coq definition), and is_prime_spec returns whether b matches the primality result. The Coq spec states b=true iff is_prime_pred(n) and b=false iff not is_prime_pred(n), which is equivalent to checking b == is_prime_pred(n) for boolean b.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification for concrete inputs. The divides function checks divisibility (handling d=0 edge case appropriately), is_prime_pred checks if n >= 2 and has no divisors from 2 to sqrt(n), and is_prime_spec returns true iff b matches the primality judgment. The Coq spec's biconditional structure (b=true iff prime, b=false iff not prime) is equivalent to checking b == is_prime_pred(n) for boolean b.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification for concrete inputs. The divides function checks divisibility (handling d=0 edge case appropriately), is_prime_pred checks if n >= 2 and has no divisors from 2 to sqrt(n), and is_prime_spec returns whether the boolean b matches the primality judgment. The Coq spec's biconditional structure (b=true iff prime, b=false iff not prime) is equivalent to checking b == is_prime_pred(n) for boolean b.

Python mirrors Coq: divides matches integer divisibility (including d=0), is_prime_pred checks n>=2 with no divisors up to floor(sqrt n), and is_prime_spec returns b equals that predicate, which is equivalent to the Coq biconditionals.

For n>=2, both check no divisors i in [2..floor(sqrt(n))]; for n<2 both return false. divides(0,n) iff n=0 matches. is_prime_spec returns True exactly when b equals is_prime_pred(n), matching the Coq spec.

The Python functions implement the same concrete checks as the Coq specs: divides matches existence of k (including the d=0 case), is_prime_pred returns False for n<2 and otherwise checks for divisors up to floor(sqrt n), and is_prime_spec returns True exactly when b matches the primality predicate. Behavior aligns for all integer inputs.
